name: Binary Search
coreIntuition: Binary search is NOT about 'searching a sorted array'. It's about **eliminating half the search space at each step using a monotonic predicate**. Any time you can define a yes/no question where the answer flips from false to true (or vice versa) across an ordered space, binary search applies — even if there's no array involved at all.
mentalModel: Imagine you're playing a guessing game: 'I'm thinking of a number between 1 and 1000.' You don't guess randomly — you guess 500, then based on 'higher/lower', you halve the remaining range. Now generalize: the 'number' can be anything (a capacity, a distance, a time), and the 'higher/lower' is your predicate function. Binary search is just this game formalized.
whenToUse: You need to find an exact value or boundary in a sorted/monotonic space
The problem asks for 'minimum X such that…' or 'maximum X such that…'
You can define a predicate (canAchieve, isValid, isFeasible) that is monotonically true/false
The brute force is linear search over possible answers, and you notice a monotonic property
The problem mentions sorted arrays, rotated arrays, or searching

thinkingFramework.detail: What are ALL possible answers? This could be array indices [0, n-1], a value range [1, max], or even a continuous domain [0.0, 1e9]. Don't limit yourself to array indices.

Write a function canAchieve(x) or isValid(x) that returns true/false. This is the hardest and most important part — if you can define this clearly, the binary search is trivial. Example: 'Can I ship all packages in D days with capacity x?' → true/false.
Prove to yourself that the predicate flips exactly once across the search space. If canAchieve(5) = true, is canAchieve(6) also true? If yes, you have monotonicity and binary search works.
Decide: are you looking for the FIRST true, or the LAST false? This determines whether you set lo=mid+1 or hi=mid. Pick one convention and stick with it — inconsistency causes bugs.

thinkingFramework.step: 5. Handle edge cases by tracing small inputs
thinkingFramework.detail: Always trace your loop with a 2-element search space [lo, lo+1]. Does it terminate? Does it pick the right element? This catches off-by-one bugs before they happen.

commonTraps.trap: Off-by-one errors with lo, hi, and mid
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Use a consistent template. If you use lo < hi with hi = mid and lo = mid + 1, the answer is at lo when the loop ends. Trace through a 2-element case every time.

commonTraps.trap: Not recognizing binary search applies (especially 'binary search on the answer')
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Key phrases: 'minimize the maximum', 'maximum the minimum', 'find the smallest X such that', 'what's the minimum capacity/speed/value'. These almost always signal binary search on the answer.
Use mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2 instead of (lo + hi) / 2 to prevent overflow in languages without arbitrary precision.

commonTraps.trap: Infinite loop when lo = hi - 1 and mid rounds down to lo
commonTraps.howToAvoid: If you use lo = mid (not mid + 1), you need mid = lo + (hi - lo + 1) / 2 (round up) to make progress. Or restructure to always use lo = mid + 1.

interviewVariantStrategy: Interviewers have 3 favorite ways to disguise binary search: (1) **Binary search on the answer** — instead of searching an array, you're searching over possible answer values. 'Minimum capacity to ship packages in D days' → binary search over capacities. (2) **Rotated/modified arrays** — the sorted property is disrupted, but you can still eliminate half by checking which half is sorted. (3) **2D search** — searching in a row-sorted and column-sorted matrix. For all variants, the approach is the same: define the search space, define the predicate, verify monotonicity. The format changes; the thinking doesn't.

resources.title: Topcoder — Binary Search as Predicate Search
resources.why: The best explanation of binary search as a predicate search — transforms how you think about it

resources.title: Errichto — Binary Search on the Answer
resources.why: Clear walkthrough of the 'search the answer space' technique with multiple contest problems

resources.title: CP-Algorithms — Binary Search
resources.why: Formal treatment with correct implementation templates and edge case analysis

resources.title: VisuAlgo — Binary Search
resources.why: Step through binary search visually to understand how the search space shrinks
